---
title: "Sect 1.3 and 1.4 R Commands Help Sheet"
author: "MATH 365"
editor: visual
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc_depth: 2
    number-sections: false
    number-depth: 2
    theme: flatly
    embed-resources: true
---

## Basic Summary Functions

These base R functions provide quick ways to summarize numeric data. We'll use `mtcars$mpg` (miles per gallon) as our example:

``` r
x <- mtcars$mpg
```

### `mean()` – Arithmetic Average

``` r
mean(x)  # Average value
```

### `sd()` – Standard Deviation

``` r
sd(x)    # Measure of typical distance from the mean
```

### `range()` – Minimum and Maximum

``` r
range(x)           # Returns a vector: c(min, max)
diff(range(x))     # Computes the range as a single number
```

### `summary()` – Five-Number Summary + Mean

``` r
summary(x)  # Min, 1st Qu., Median, Mean, 3rd Qu., Max
```

### `fivenum()` – Tukey's Five-Number Summary

``` r
fivenum(x)  # Min, Lower Hinge, Median, Upper Hinge, Max
```

### `mean(..., trim = ...)` – Trimmed Mean

``` r
mean(x, trim = 0.1)  # Trimmed mean after removing 10% from each end
```

-   Useful when you want a **robust average** that’s less affected by outliers
-   Common choices: 5%, 10%, or 20% trimming depending on context

## Visual Summary: `boxplot()`

``` r
boxplot(x, main = "Boxplot of mtcars$mpg")
```

-   The box shows the **hinges** (Tukey's lower and upper fourths) and the **median**.
-   Whiskers extend to min and max (or only to non-outliers, depending on method).

## Computing the Hinges and Fourth Spread

The output from `fivenum()` gives:

``` r
f <- fivenum(x)
f
```

This returns: - $f_1$ = minimum - $f_2$ = lower hinge (approx. 1st quartile) - $f_3$ = median - $f_4$ = upper hinge (approx. 3rd quartile) - $f_5$ = maximum

### Hinges and Fourth Spread

``` r
lower_hinge <- f[2]  # Lower fourth / hinge
upper_hinge <- f[4]  # Upper fourth / hinge

fourth_spread <- upper_hinge - lower_hinge
fourth_spread
```

This value represents the **spread of the middle half** of the data and is robust to outliers.


------------------------------------------------------------------------


## Comparing Groups

These tools summarize or display a numeric variable broken down by a
categorical grouping variable. We'll use the cereal dataset with
`Sodium` as the numeric variable and `Cereal` (values `"G"`,
`"K"`, `"P"`) as the grouping variable.

``` r
cereal_data <- read.csv("http://people.kzoo.edu/enordmoe/math365/data/exp01-21.csv")
```

### `tapply()` – One Statistic per Group

``` r
tapply(cereal_data$Sodium, cereal_data$Cereal, mean)
tapply(cereal_data$Sodium, cereal_data$Cereal, sd)
tapply(cereal_data$Sodium, cereal_data$Cereal, median)
```

-   Returns a compact named vector — one value per group
-   Works with any function: `mean`, `sd`, `median`, `fivenum`, etc.

### `by()` – Full Summary per Group

``` r
by(cereal_data$Sodium, cereal_data$Cereal, summary)
```

-   Prints a readable six-number summary for each group separately
-   Good for a quick look at all groups at once

### `aggregate()` – Summary Table as a Data Frame

``` r
aggregate(Sodium ~ Cereal, data = cereal_data,
          FUN = function(x) c(mean = mean(x), sd = sd(x)))
```

-   Returns results as a  data frame;  easy to read and export
-   Use the formula interface `y ~ group` to specify response and
    grouping variable

### Side-by-Side Boxplots

``` r
boxplot(Sodium ~ Cereal, data = cereal_data,
        main = "Sodium Content by Manufacturer",
        xlab = "Manufacturer", ylab = "Sodium (mg)",
        names = c("General Mills", "Kellogg", "Post"),
        col = c("skyblue", "forestgreen", "tomato"))
```

-   The formula `Sodium ~ Cereal` tells R to plot `Sodium` separately
    for each level of `Cereal`
-   `names` overrides the axis labels so `"G"`, `"K"`, `"P"` display
    as full manufacturer names

### Side-by-Side Histograms

``` r
op <- par(no.readonly = TRUE)
par(mfrow = c(3, 1), mar = c(4, 4, 2, 1))

# Find the overall range of the Sodium variable
xlims <- range(cereal_data$Sodium)

hist(cereal_data$Sodium[cereal_data$Cereal == "G"],
     main = "General Mills", xlab = "Sodium (mg)",
     xlim = xlims, col = "skyblue")
hist(cereal_data$Sodium[cereal_data$Cereal == "K"],
     main = "Kellogg", xlab = "Sodium (mg)",
     xlim = xlims, col = "forestgreen")
hist(cereal_data$Sodium[cereal_data$Cereal == "P"],
     main = "Post", xlab = "Sodium (mg)",
     xlim = xlims, col = "tomato")

par(op)
```

-   `mfrow = c(3, 1)` stacks three plots vertically
-   Using a common `xlims` makes the three panels directly comparable
-   `par(op)` restores the original graphics settings afterward
